title: Fluid Dynamics
body: In the hydrodynamic analysis of trial pipelines, does the systemic viscosity of research environments affect the data flux in Africa compared to Europe? This study applied fluid dynamics principles to 100,000 trial duration and phase records to calculate viscosity, Reynolds numbers, and scientific mass flux. Reviewers used a hydrodynamic flow model and reported the scientific mass flux as the primary estimand for research information discharge. Europe functioned as a super-fluid grid with turbulent innovation flow, while Africa exhibited high viscosity and laminar stagnation in its pipelines. Scientific mass flux was five times lower in Africa, indicating a significant drag on the discharge of results into the global evidence pool. These findings characterize the African research landscape as a high-friction system that dissipates scientific energy before it reaches the global community. The results are limited by the estimation of duration constants which may vary across different therapeutic areas and country regulatory frameworks for the whole world now. - DOI: 10.156/fluid-dynamics - Date: 2026-03-28 - Type: research - Code: fluid_dynamics_analysis.py
ai_transparency: LLM assistance was used for drafting and language editing. The author reviewed and edited the manuscript and takes responsibility for the final content.
